title: Age limit for lawyers commissioned as notaries
text:
Headnotes

to the Judgment of the First Senate of 23 September 2025

- 1 BvR 1796/23 -

Age limit for lawyers commissioned as notaries

Occupational freedom entails both an economic dimension and a
dimension related to the development of one’s personality. It gives
definition to the fundamental right to the free development of one’s
personality in the area of individual achievement and creating and
earning a livelihood and aims to have the least amount of regulation of
occupational activity possible.

Given the sustained shortage of lawyers commissioned as notaries
and the current knowledge on how age affects professional faculties, the
age limit of 70 years pursuant to § 47 no. 2, first alternative and
§ 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries only achieves the objectives
pursued by such rule – the proper functioning of the administration of
justice, the (fair) distribution of career opportunities among
generations and the protection of the administration of justice from
risks that may arise from age-related decline in faculties among
notaries – to a very limited extent. It disproportionately restricts
occupational freedom insofar as it concerns lawyers commissioned as
notaries.

FEDERAL CONSTITUTIONAL COURT

- 1 BvR 1796/23 -

IN THE NAME OF THE PEOPLE

In the proceedings

on

the constitutional complaint

of Mr (...),

- authorised representative:
Prof. Dr. Gregor Thüsing, LL.M. (Harvard),

(…) -

1. directly against

a) the Order of the Federal Court of Justice

of 24 July 2024 - NotZ 1/23 -,

b) the Order of the Federal Court of Justice

of 13 November 2023 - NotZ(Brfg) 4/22 -,

c) the Judgment of the Federal Court of Justice

of 7 August 2023 - NotZ(Brfg) 4/22 -,

d) the Judgment of the Cologne Higher Regional Court

of 10 February 2022 - Not 5/21 -,

2. indirectly against

§ 47 no. 2 first alternative, § 48a of the Federal Code for
Notaries

the Federal Constitutional Court – First Senate –

with the participation of
Justices

President
Harbarth,

Ott,

Christ,

Radtke,

Härtel,

Wolff,

Eifert,

Meßling

held on the basis of the oral hearing of 25 March 2025:

Judgment:

§ 47 no. 2 first alternative of the Federal Code for Notaries as
amended by the Act Implementing the Professional Qualifications
Directive and Amending Other Provisions Concerning Providers of Legal
Advice of 12 May 2017 (Federal Law Gazette I p. 1121) and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries as amended by the Act Amending the
Professional Regulations for Notaries and Lawyers of 29 January 1991
(Federal Law Gazette I p. 150) are incompatible with Art. 12(1) of the
Basic Law to the extent that they concern lawyers commissioned as
notaries.

The provisions that have been declared incompatible with the
Basic Law shall continue to apply until 30 June 2026, including for
lawyers commissioned as notaries.

For the rest, the constitutional complaint is rejected.

[…]

Reasons:

The proceedings concern the question of whether the statutory age
limit of 70 years for notaries pursuant to § 47 no. 2 first alternative
and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries (Bundesnotarordnung
– BNotO) is compatible with the Basic Law (Grundgesetz – GG)
insofar as it concerns lawyers commissioned as notaries
(Anwaltsnotariat).

1. The legal framework governing the profession of notary is laid
down in the Federal Code for Notaries (Federal Law Gazette,
Bundesgesetzblatt – BGBl I p. 98), last amended by Art. 12 of
the Act of 23 October 2024 (BGBl I no. 323 p. 9).

a) Pursuant to § 1 of the Federal Code for Notaries, notaries are
independent holders of a public office. They are appointed in the
Länder to certify legal transactions and to perform other tasks
in the field of the administration of justice. Their functions include
notarial acts pursuant to §§ 20 to 22 of the Federal Code for Notaries
in particular. These include notarial recording and authentication (§ 20
Federal Code for Notaries), certain certifications (§ 21 Federal Code
for Notaries), the administration of oaths and declarations in lieu of
oath (§ 22 Federal Code for Notaries). The office of notary also
encompasses the provision of custodial services, in the form of the
safekeeping and delivery of valuables (§ 23 Federal Code for Notaries),
and the provision of other advisory services in the area of the
administration of justice to the parties involved, in particular the
drawing up of draft documents and providing advice to the parties
involved (§ 24 Federal Code for Notaries).

b) Notaries are not part of the public service. While they practise a
public profession, they perform their tasks as officeholders in private
practice (cf. Decisions of the Federal Constitutional Court,
Entscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE 131, 130
<139>).

c) The practice of a notary is localised. Notaries are assigned to a
specific location where they must maintain their office (§ 10 Federal
Code for Notaries). A notary’s jurisdiction is the district overseen by
the Local Court in which that notary has their office (§ 10a(1) Federal
Code for Notaries), and their official district is the district overseen
by the Higher Regional Court in which that notary has their office
(§ 11(1) Federal Code for Notaries). Notaries are to perform their
notarial acts within their jurisdiction (cf. § 10a(2) Federal Code for
Notaries). Outside their official district, they may only operate under
strict conditions (cf. § 11(2) Federal Code for Notaries). That said,
the notarial acts performed by notaries need not be linked to their
jurisdiction or official district; clients are generally free to choose
a notary anywhere Germany ([…]).

d) While the exercise of the office of notary is as such uniformly
governed by federal law, the organisational rules regarding the
appointment of notaries differ across the Länder in accordance
with federal law; there are two types of notaries.

aa) Pursuant to § 3 in conjunction with §§ 114 and 116 of the Federal
Code for Notaries, notaries can either hold such office as their primary
occupation (notaries only) or may concurrently practice as a lawyer and
hold the office of notary (lawyers commissioned as notaries). The
Länder in which notaries can only hold this office as their
primary occupation include Bavaria, Brandenburg, Hamburg,
Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania, Rhineland-Palatinate, Saarland, Saxony,
Saxony-Anhalt and Thuringia. The system of lawyers commissioned as
notaries is used in Berlin, Bremen, Hesse, Lower Saxony and
Schleswig-Holstein. In Baden-Württemberg, notaries who hold this office
as their primary occupation are appointed (§ 144(1) first sentence
Federal Code for Notaries); lawyers who were commissioned as notaries as
of 31 December 2017 remain in office (§ 116(1) Federal Code for
Notaries). In North Rhine-Westphalia, in the district overseen by the
Cologne Higher Regional Court and in parts of the district overseen by
the Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court, notaries must hold this office as
their primary occupation; for all other districts – like in the
complainant’s Local Court district– lawyers are commissioned as
notaries.

bb) The two types of notaries are not subject to different
professional regulations when it comes to the exercise of their office.
However, the Federal Code for Notaries provides for legal differences
relating to the organisational rules.

(1) Notaries who hold such office as their primary occupation may not
practise any other occupation, they may also not be admitted to the bar
(§ 8(2) first sentence Federal Code for Notaries). According to the
legislative concept, for lawyers commissioned as notaries, the office of
notary is only a secondary occupation in addition to their primary
occupation of lawyer (§ 3(2) Federal Code for Notaries). Lawyers
commissioned as notaries must therefore always be members of the bar
(cf. § 3(2) Federal Code for Notaries, § 47 no. 4 Federal Code for
Notaries) and may practise certain other occupations (§ 8(2) second
sentence Federal Code for Notaries).

(2) One important difference concerns admission to the profession.
Both types of notaries must have the personal and professional aptitude
for the office (§ 5(1) Federal Code for Notaries). The maximum age for
the initial appointment as notary is 60 years for both types of notaries
pursuant to § 5(4) of the Federal Code for Notaries. The other
prerequisites differ. […]

(3) A newly appointed notary who holds this office as their primary
occupation usually succeeds a notary who left office and takes over
their personnel and resources. Often, they also take over their
predecessor’s clients. By contrast, in accordance with administrative
practice for lawyers commissioned as notaries, such notaries do not have
a successor. Their notary practice is liquidated when they leave office
([…]).

(4) […]

e) The number of notary positions is determined by § 4 of the Federal
Code for Notaries. Pursuant to that provision, the number of notaries to
be appointed is determined by what is necessary to meet the needs of the
orderly administration of justice (§ 4 first sentence Federal Code for
Notaries). In particular, it must be ensured that those in need of legal
services are adequately supplied with notarial services and that an
appropriate age structure of the members of the profession is maintained
(age structure clause; § 4 second sentence Federal Code for
Notaries).

In accordance with § 1 and § 12(1) of the Federal Code for Notaries,
making such determination and appointing notaries falls within the
competence of the Länder; with the number of appointments left
to the duly exercised discretion of the Länder (cf. BVerfGE 73,
280 <294>; Federal Court of Justice, Order of 20 July 2020 - NotZ
(Brfg) 5/19 -, para. 4). The need for appointments is regularly
determined by the Land departments of justice based on the
volume of notarial transactions in the respective Local Court district.
They use reference values to determine such need. […]

In the past few years, it has been determined that the need for
lawyers commissioned as notaries has risen in some Länder.
[…]

2. Pursuant to § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal
Code for Notaries, the office of notary expires upon reaching 70 years
of age, both for notaries practising primarily as such and for lawyers
commissioned as notaries. These provisions, which the constitutional
complaint has indirectly challenged, read as follows:

§ 47 of the Federal Code for Notaries – Expiry of office

The office of notary expires upon (...)

2. reaching retirement age (§ 48a) or death (…).

§ 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries – Age limit

Notaries reach retirement age at the end of that month in which they
reach the age of 70.

3. The legislator inserted these provisions with the Act Amending the
Professional Regulations for Notaries and Lawyers of 29 January 1991
(BGBl I p. 150). At the same time, the age limit for the initial
appointment as notary (presently § 5(4) Federal Code for Notaries) and
the age structure clause in § 4 second sentence were inserted into the
Federal Code for Notaries.

a) The original version of the Federal Code for Notaries contained
neither age limits nor other age-related provisions. However, pursuant
to § 113(II) subsection (1) of the Federal Code for Notaries, a notary
practising primarily as such in the area of activity of the Notaries’
Fund (Notarkasse) in Bavaria and in the then administrative
district of Palatinate could be removed from office once they had turned
70. The administrative practice of some Länder to set age
limits for applicants for a commission as notary was ended by a decision
of the Federal Constitutional Court because there was no statutory basis
for such a practice at the time (cf. BVerfGE 80, 257).

The Ordinance of the Council of Ministers of the German Democratic
Republic on Notaries in Private Practice of 20 June 1990 (Law Gazette,
Gesetzblatt – GBl. I no. 37 p. 475), which continued to apply
in the East German Länder until 7 September 1998 in accordance
with the Unification Treaty, likewise did not provide for an age limit
for the expiry of the office of notary, even though it did exclude
applicants over 60 pursuant to the conditions of § 5(2) second sentence
of the Ordinance.

b) The 1991 amendment was based on a draft act by the Federal
Government, according to which the prerequisites to become a lawyer
commissioned as notary were to be revised in view of demographic
developments. Given the marked increase in the number of lawyers, the
legislator saw a risk that there would be an excess of lawyers
commissioned as notaries in the foreseeable future, which would be
incompatible with the public office (cf. Bundestag document,
Bundestagsdrucksache – BTDrucks 11/6007, p. 1).

aa) The legislator argued that while the departments of justice
could, pursuant to § 4(2) of the Federal Code for Notaries in the
version of 24 February 1961, make the appointment as a lawyer
commissioned as notary contingent on the formal criterion of the expiry
of a waiting period, controlling access almost exclusively through
waiting periods had turned out to be problematic. According to the
legislator, the approach of generally granting any lawyer a commission
as notary after the expiry of the waiting period could not be continued.
Otherwise, individual lawyers commissioned as notaries would receive
such a small number of notarial transactions that they would be
practising in the essential areas of a notarial office without adequate
experience and could no longer generate the financial basis for the
performance of notarial duties. According to the legislator, this would
endanger the proper administration of justice. Therefore, § 4 of the
Federal Code for Notaries should be amended in such a way that the
waiting period in itself would no longer be a suitable criterion to
determine the need for appointment. However, it was to remain the
standard prerequisite for the appointment of lawyers commissioned as
notaries (cf. BTDrucks 11/6007, p. 8 f.). The possibility of making the
appointment of lawyers commissioned as notaries solely contingent on the
expiry of a waiting period, which had previously been set out in § 4(2)
of the Federal Code for Notaries, was eliminated in the draft act
submitted in 1991.

bb) In addition, the draft act contained an age limit for the initial
appointment as notary (§ 6(1) second sentence government draft of the
Federal Code for Notaries; cf. BTDrucks 11/6007, p. 4). The explanatory
memorandum states the following (BTDrucks 11/6007, p. 10):

Based on the practice of several Länder (North
Rhine-Westphalia: 60 years of age, Hesse, 62 years of age, Lower Saxony:
62 years of age), the second sentence provides for an age limit for the
appointment as notary. The introduction of an age limit of 60 years
serves to ensure continuity by counteracting frequent changes in the
notarial office, including in consideration of the greater difficulties
of adjustment to the profession of notary associated with advanced age.
At the same time, the provision serves to address the risk of an ageing
profession. Since the aspect of adjustment is not relevant in cases
where a former notary wants to be reappointed, or wants to be appointed
in a different location, the age limit is only to apply to initial
appointments.

c) In the parliamentary deliberations, members of Parliament
expressed the concern that the stricter rules for entering the
profession provided for in the draft act could block access to the
position of lawyer commissioned as notary for a considerable time (cf.
Bundestag Minutes of plenary proceedings,
BT-Plenarprotokoll 11/194, pp. 14895 f.).

aa) For this reason, the recommendation of the Committee on Legal
Affairs that was ultimately adopted by the German Bundestag
added to the draft act an age limit of 70 years, after which the
notarial office would expire, pursuant to § 47 no. 1 first alternative
of the Federal Code for Notaries (former version) and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries (cf. BTDrucks 11/8307, p. 7). The reason given
for the introduction of a maximum age for practising the profession of
notary as a public office was the objective of maintaining an
appropriate age structure, in particular for lawyers commissioned as
notaries (cf. BTDrucks 11/8307, p. 18).

According to a transitional provision (Art. 3 Amendment Act),
notaries who were 58 when the act entered into force could remain in
office for another twelve years.

bb) The recommendation also added the age-related provision of § 4
second sentence of the Federal Code for Notaries to the draft act,
according to which the objective of maintaining an appropriate age
structure must be taken into account when notaries are appointed. The
explanatory memorandum to the recommendation specifically emphasised the
objective of an appropriate age structure to address the concern that
the new rules for entering the profession would block access to the
profession of lawyer commissioned as notary for a considerable time (cf.
BTDrucks 11/8307, p. 17).

At the same time, the possibility of making the appointment of
lawyers commissioned as notaries solely contingent on the expiry of a
waiting period, which had previously been set out in § 4(2) of the
Federal Code for Notaries in the version of 24 February 1961, was
eliminated, as provided for in the Federal Government’s draft act.

d) The provisions entered into force on 3 February 1991 in the
territory in which the Federal Code of Notaries had previously applied,
i.e. the former federal territory and the Land Berlin,
including former East Berlin. In the East German Länder, the
Federal Code for Notaries replaced the Ordinance on Notaries in Private
Practice with effect from 8 September 1998 (cf. Art. 13(1) of the Third
Act Amending the Federal Code for Notaries and Other Acts of 31 August
1998; BGBl I p. 2585), which meant that the provisions also applied in
this area as of the same date. However, a transitional provision stated
that notaries appointed in the East German Länder who were 58
when the Act entered into force could remain in office for another
twelve years (Art. 13(9) of the Act). Due to subsequent changes in the
numbering of the grounds for expiry in § 47 of the Federal Code for
Notaries, the retirement age is now set out in § 47 no. 2 first
alternative (cf. BGBl I 2017 p. 1121 <1146>).

4. The new age limit under § 47 no. 1 first alternative of the
Federal Code for Notaries (former version) and § 48a of the Federal Code
for Notaries was the subject of a constitutional complaint. By order of
29 October 1992 - 1 BvR 1581/91 -, the Second Chamber of the First
Senate of the Federal Constitutional Court did not admit the
constitutional complaint for decision. It held that the interference
with occupational freedom under Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law was
constitutionally justified. The provision served to further an
especially significant interest of the common good – achieving an
appropriate age structure within the notarial profession in the interest
of the proper functioning of the administration of justice. Clients
should be able to choose from notaries of different ages who have a
minimum level of professional experience gained through the number and
types of notarial transactions conducted by them in all areas of
notarial practice.

5. a) In Germany, 6,346 notaries were practising as of 1 January
2025, of whom 1,700 worked as notaries as their primary occupation and
4,646 were lawyers commissioned as notaries. The number of notaries
practising primarily as such is largely stable. As of 1 January 2012,
this number was 1,535, as of 1 January 2018, it was 1,719, with the
increase resulting from the fact that, on 1 January 2018, approximately
300 notaries practicing in Baden-Württemberg were appointed as notaries
for whom this is the primary occupation due to a change in the law (cf.
§ 114(2) Federal Code for Notaries). By contrast, the number of lawyers
commissioned as notaries has dwindled. On 1 January 2012, 6,122 lawyers
were commissioned as notaries, on 1 January 2018, the number was 5,469;
currently (see the above considerations), there are 4,646 lawyers
commissioned as notaries.

b) Among the notaries for whom this is the primary occupation, 23%
were 60 to 69 years old, 32% were 50 to 59 years old, 31% were 40 to 49
years old, and 14% were younger than 40 in 2024. Among the lawyers
commissioned as notaries, 31% were 60 to 69 years old, 35% were 50 to 59
years old, 28% were 40 to 49 years old, and 5% were younger than 40.

c) The number of candidates applying for the office of notary differs
depending on the type of notary.

For the office of notary as a primary occupation, the number of
applicants for the position of trainee notary is the determinative
factor. There are more than enough applicants in nearly every
Land. In several Länder where notary is a primary
occupation, the number of applicants considerably exceeds the open
positions for trainee notaries, for example in Hamburg, where in 2024
there were 54 applicants for three positions. The situation is different
in Brandenburg, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt and Thuringia, where the number of
applicants has dwindled, with the number of applicants falling below the
number of open positions in some years.

bb) In the case of lawyers commissioned as notaries, the number of
applicants has for some years fallen below the number of open positions.
As early as 2012, there was a shortage of applicants in the majority of
districts overseen by the respective Higher Regional Court.

In subsequent years, this shortage became further entrenched and
intensified […].

In 2024, the shortage of applicants continued […].

 

1. The complainant, who was born in 1953, was admitted to the bar in
1983. In 1992, he was appointed as a lawyer commissioned as notary. He
was assigned an office in Dinslaken, in the district overseen by the
Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court. His notarial office expired on 30
November 2023 due to the statutory age limit of 70. In the meantime, the
complainant has been appointed as the permanent deputy notary
(Notarvertreter) for his law partner, who is also a lawyer
commissioned as notary.

2. a) In 2021, the complainant filed a lawsuit with the Cologne
Higher Regional Court against the President of the Düsseldorf Higher
Regional Court as the supervisory authority. He applied for a
declaration that his notarial office had not expired upon his reaching
the age limit. He asserted that the statutory age limit violated
Art. 21(1) of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union
and the prohibition of discrimination on grounds of age under Art. 1 and
Art. 2(2a) of Directive 2000/78/EC. He argued that the age limit was no
longer necessary, and therefore not justified, due to a shortage of
applicants for notary positions that had occurred in recent years. By
judgment of 10 February 2022, the Cologne Higher Regional Court rejected
the lawsuit as unfounded.

b) The Federal Court of Justice rejected the complainant’s appeal
against this decision by judgment of 7 August 2023. […]

-44

[…]

c) The complaint seeking remedy for a violation of the right to be
heard (Anhörungsrüge) lodged thereupon was rejected as
unfounded by the Federal Court of Justice in its order of 13 November
2023.

d) The action for annulment lodged against the judgment on the
grounds that the Federal Court of Justice failed to comply with its duty
to refer the matter to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU)
under Art. 267(3) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
(TFEU) was dismissed as inadmissible by the Federal Court of Justice in
its order of 24 July 2024. […]

3. The constitutional complaint directly challenges the judgment and
the orders of the Federal Court of Justice and the judgment of the
Cologne Higher Regional Court. It indirectly challenges § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries.

The complainant asserts that the judgments of the Federal Court of
Justice and the Cologne Higher Regional Court directly violate his
rights under Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law, and that § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries indirectly
violate these rights. […]

Moreover, in accordance with his submissions regarding Art. 12(1) of
the Basic Law, the complainant asserts that his rights under Art. 15(1)
of the Charter of Fundamental Rights, or, subsidiarily, Art. 16 of the
Charter, have been violated. He also claims that the challenged
judgments and the provision violate the prohibition on discrimination on
grounds of age guaranteed in Art. 21(1) of the Charter and specified in
the area of employment and occupation by Directive 2000/78/EC. The
complainant asserts that he is subject to unjustified unequal treatment
due to his age.

He also claims that the judgment of the Federal Court of Justice
violates his right to his lawful judge under Art. 101(1) second sentence
of the Basic Law. He contends that the Federal Court of Justice would
have had to request a preliminary ruling under Art. 267(3) TFEU. […]

He also alleges that the judgment of the Federal Court of Justice
violates his right to be heard under Art. 103(1) of the Basic Law.
[…]

The complainant extends his claim of a violation of Art. 101(1)
second sentence and Art. 103(1) of the Basic Law – rights that are
equivalent to fundamental rights – to the judgment of the Cologne Higher
Regional Court. He also considers the orders of the Federal Court of
Justice of 13 November 2023 and 24 July 2024 to be in violation of
Art. 103(1) of the Basic Law.

 

The complainant lodged an application for a preliminary injunction
pursuant to § 32 of the Federal Constitutional Court Act
(Bundesverfassungsgerichtsgesetz – BVerfGG) together with his
constitutional complaint, requesting that the expiry of his notarial
office be provisionally suspended until a decision was rendered in the
principal proceedings. By order of 18 October 2023, the Third Chamber of
the First Senate of the Federal Constitutional Court rejected the
application for a preliminary injunction because the complainant had not
substantiated grounds of such special weight as to justify the
suspension of a law.

 

The opportunity to submit statements was given to the German
Bundestag, the Bundesrat, the Federal Government, all
Land governments, the Federal Court of Justice, the defendant
in the initial proceedings, and – as expert third parties within the
meaning of § 27a of the Federal Constitutional Court Act – to the
Federal Anti-Discrimination Agency (Antidiskriminierungsstelle des
Bundes), the Federal Association of Notaries
(Bundesnotarkammer), the Federal Bar Association
(Bundesrechtsanwaltskammer), the Institute for the Legal
Profession (Institut für Anwaltsrecht) at the University of
Cologne, the Federal Association of Corporate Law Firms in Germany
(Bundesverband der Wirtschaftskanzleien in Deutschland e.V.),
the German Bar Association (Deutscher Anwaltsverein e.V.), the
German Association for Gerontology and Geriatrics (Deutsche
Gesellschaft für Gerontologie und Geriatrie e.V.), the Association
of German Notaries (Deutscher Notarverein e.V.), the German
Centre for Age-Related Questions (Deutsches Zentrum für Altersfragen
e.V.) and the Federation of German Consumer Organisations
(Verbraucherzentrale Bundesverband e.V.). In preparation of the
oral hearing, the Senate sent a questionnaire to certain parties
entitled to submit a statement and expert third parties. The following
entities submitted a statement:

[…] The Federal Ministry of Justice on behalf of the Federal
Government, [the Land governments of Bavaria and Saxony,
several Land governments from Länder where lawyers are
commissioned as notaries, the defendant in the initial proceedings, the
Federal Association of Notaries, the Federal Bar Association, the
Federal Association of Corporate Law Firms in Germany, the German Bar
Association, the Association of German Notaries, the Federal
Anti-Discrimination Agency, the Institute for the Legal Profession at
the University of Cologne and the German Centre for Age-Related
Questions and the German Association for Gerontology and
Geriatrics].

-71

[…]

The Federal Constitutional Court conducted an oral hearing on 25
March 2025. The complainant supplemented and expanded upon his
submissions. The Federal Government, the Land government of
Lower Saxony and the defendant in the initial proceedings were also
heard. The expert third parties who made a statement at the oral hearing
were the Federal Anti-Discrimination Agency, the Association of German
Notaries, the Institute for the Legal Profession at the University of
Cologne, the German Centre for Age-Related Questions and the German
Association for Gerontology and Geriatrics.

 

The constitutional complaint is admissible to the extent that the
claimed violation of Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law directly challenges the
judgment of 7 August 2023 of the Federal Court of Justice and indirectly
challenges § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code
for Notaries. With regard to the other asserted fundamental rights
violations, the complainant failed to sufficiently substantiate that he
has standing. The constitutional complaint is inadmissible to the extent
that it is directed against the judgment of 10 February 2022 of the
Cologne Higher Regional Court and against the orders of 13 November 2023
and 24 July 2024 of the Federal Court of Justice.

In addition to the directly challenged decisions of the Federal Court
of Justice and of the Cologne Higher Regional Court, the constitutional
complaint indirectly challenges the age limit under § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries, but only insofar
as it applies to lawyers commissioned as notaries (see 1. below). The
provision can be divided in this way (see 2. below).

1. The limitation of the indirect challenge in the constitutional
complaint results from the fact that the complainant expressly asserts
the unconstitutionality of the rule for lawyers commissioned as notaries
only, claiming that it should be partially invalid in relation to
lawyers commissioned as notaries.

2. The challenge can be limited in this manner because the
application of the rule can be divided between notaries for whom this is
the primary occupation on the one hand and for lawyers commissioned as
notaries on the other.

a) The Federal Constitutional Court has recognised in its case-law
that provisions can also be declared void or incompatible with the Basic
Law with regard to specific applications only (cf. BVerfGE 12, 296; cf.
regarding Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law BVerfGE 117, 163). The only case
in which a provision may not be declared void or incompatible in part is
if this would lead to a law that is by its own terms incomprehensible
and thus does not satisfy the principles of legal certainty and clarity
(cf. BVerfGE 113, 273 <315 f.>) or if the continued application of
individual provisions cannot be considered due to the uniform
legislative concept (cf. BVerfGE 111, 226 <273>).

The rule imposed by § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries can be divided in such a way that it can be
declared void or incompatible with the Basic Law to the extent that it
applies to lawyers commissioned as notaries. This does not affect legal
certainty and clarity because it can be clearly determined which
notaries are lawyers commissioned as notaries, and are thus affected by
a partial declaration of voidness or incompatibility. The possibility of
dividing the provision is also not precluded by a uniform legislative
concept. It is true that the legislator, in the historical development
of notary law, sought to harmonise the many different regional notarial
codes. Accordingly, the Federal Code for Notaries is based on the
assumption of the uniformity of the notarial profession. However, the
legislator has only fully harmonised the exercise of the notarial
office. By contrast, the age limit forms part of the organisational
rules governing the profession, which differ considerably for the two
types of notaries (cf. para. 6 ff.).

The Federal Constitutional Court has jurisdiction to review the
indirectly challenged provisions on the basis of the fundamental rights
of the Basic Law. It is true that the age limit falls within the scope
of application of a legal act of the European Union: Directive
2000/78/EC, whose purpose, according to its Art. 1, is to combat
discrimination on the grounds of age among others. This directive is
also applicable to the profession of notary (cf. CJEU, Ministero
della Giustizia, 3 June 2021, C-914/19, EU:C:2021:430). However,
the age limit is not a provision that implements binding EU law
(regarding the standard cf. BVerfGE 155, 119 <162 ff. para. 83
ff.> – Subscriber data II; 156, 11 <35 ff. para. 63
ff.> – Counter-Terrorism Database Act II; 158, 170 <183
para. 23> – IT security vulnerabilities; 169, 332 <356
para. 53> – Federal Criminal Police Office Act II). § 47
no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries does
not implement fully harmonised EU law on the basis of an implementation
order, nor does it serve to adapt German legislation to binding EU law.
While the directive prohibits discrimination on grounds of age, it
neither precludes statutory age limits for certain occupations, nor does
it impose obligations to introduce age limits or design them in specific
ways. Rather, according to the established case-law of the Court of
Justice of the European Union, Art. 6(1) of the Directive affords Member
States broad leeway to determine age limits for entering and practising
an occupation (cf. CJEU, Fuchs und Köhler, 21 July 2011,
C-159/10, C-160/10, EU:C:2011:508, para. 61; Rechtsanwältin und
Notarin, 17 October 2024, C-408/23, EU:C:2024:901, para. 36).

The complainant has standing to the extent that he asserts that the
judgment of the Federal Court of Justice of 7 August 2023 directly
violates his fundamental right under Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law, and
that § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for
Notaries indirectly violate this right. The rest of the complainant’s
submissions do not meet the substantiation requirements.

-96

[…]

1. The complainant has exhausted the legal remedies within the
meaning of § 90(2) first sentence of the Federal Constitutional Court
Act. He has duly exhausted all avenues of appeal before the ordinary
courts.

2. Moreover, the constitutional complaint satisfies the principle of
subsidiarity.

-100

[…]

Complainants must have a recognised legal interest in bringing
constitutional complaint proceedings at the time the Federal
Constitutional Court renders its decision (cf. BVerfGE 81, 138
<140>; 159, 223 <272 para. 97 f.> – Federal pandemic
emergency brake I); this is the case here. In particular, the
complainant does not cease to be affected after the expiry of his
notarial office upon reaching the age limit. The constitutional
complaint has not become moot on the merits. The interference with
fundamental rights persists, because the rule in § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries does not just
result in the office expiring, but also blocks the affected notary from
being reappointed ([…]).

The constitutional complaint is well-founded insofar as it indirectly
challenges the age limit of 70 years set out in § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries. This limit is
incompatible with Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law insofar as it relates to
lawyers commissioned as notaries. The age limit disproportionately
interferes with the occupational freedom of lawyers commissioned as
notaries. The constitutional complaint must be rejected to the extent
that it directly challenges the judgment of the Federal Court of Justice
that is based on the challenged rule. In light of the order on the
continued applicability of the age limit, the challenged judgment is
valid.

1. Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law is a uniform fundamental right that
protects the selection and exercise of an occupation (cf. BVerfGE 7, 377
<400 f.>; 161, 63 <89> – Wind farm participatory
companies). Occupational freedom entails both an economic dimension
and a dimension related to the development of one’s personality (cf.
BVerfGE 7, 377 <397>; cf. also BVerfGE 50, 290 <362>; 110,
226 <251>). It gives definition to the fundamental right to the
free development of one’s personality in the area of individual
achievement and creating and earning a livelihood. The aim of this
guarantee is to have the least amount of regulation of occupational
activity possible (cf. BVerfGE 163, 107 <134 para. 73> –
Veterinary prescription requirement). Occupational freedom also
protects secondary occupations (cf. BVerfGE 110, 304 <321>) and
also encompasses public occupations. The work of lawyers commissioned as
notaries, which, pursuant to § 3(2) of the Federal Code for Notaries, is
carried out alongside the profession of lawyer and constitutes a public
profession, falls within the scope of protection of occupational freedom
under Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law (cf. BVerfGE 131, 130
<139>).

2. The age limit under § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries interferes with this fundamental right. It is
a direct restriction on the freedom to choose one’s occupation, as the
affected persons are no longer able to work as lawyers commissioned as
notaries. As a result of the law, their occupation ends upon reaching
the age limit. They have no choice as to whether they can continue to
practise as a notary (cf. Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the
Second Chamber of the First Senate of 29 October 1992 - 1 BvR 1581/91 -,
juris, para. 5).

3. The interference is not justified under constitutional law.
Art. 12(1) second sentence of the Basic Law contains a general
limitation clause, i.e. this right may be interfered with only pursuant
to a law (cf. BVerfGE 141, 82 <98 para. 47 with further
references>; 145, 20 <67 para. 121>; established case-law). In
order to be justified under constitutional law, the indirectly
challenged rule in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal
Code for Notaries must be formally and substantively constitutional (cf.
BVerfGE 6, 32 <41>; 163, 107 <136 para. 77>).

The challenged provision is formally constitutional; in particular,
it was enacted in accordance with the legislative competences under
Art. 74(1) no. 1 of the Basic Law. However, the interference with
occupational freedom is disproportionate; it is therefore incompatible
with Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law in substantive terms to the extent that
the provision applies to lawyers commissioned as notaries.

The principle of proportionality requires that the challenged
provision pursues a legitimate purpose, that the interference is
suitable for achieving that legitimate purpose and does not go further
than is necessary in the interests of the common good, in other words,
no equally effective but less intrusive means are available. Moreover,
the provision may not impose unreasonable burdens on fundamental right
holders (cf. BVerfGE 141, 121 <133 para. 40>; 148, 40 <52 para.
30>; 155, 238 <278 para. 99>; 163, 107 <138 para. 83>).
Even a provision that was initially constitutional can become
unconstitutional if circumstances change in such a way that the
proportionality requirements are no longer met (cf. BVerfGE 132, 334
<358 para. 67>; 150, 1 <90 para. 176>; 158, 282 <346
para. 155, 366 para. 200).

Based on these standards, the indirectly challenged rule in § 47
no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries is no
longer constitutional under the present circumstances. The age limit of
70 years does serve legitimate purposes (see a) below) and is in
principle still suitable (see b) below) and necessary (see c) below) for
achieving these purposes. However, the rule is not proportionate in the
strict sense because, under the present conditions, it imposes
unreasonable burdens on fundamental right holders (see d) below).

a) The age limit in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries pursues constitutionally legitimate
purposes.

aa) Legislative interference with fundamental rights can only be
justified if the legislator pursues constitutionally legitimate
purposes. It is for the Federal Constitutional Court to review whether
this is the case. In this respect, the Court is not limited to
considering only the purposes expressly named by the legislator itself
(cf. BVerfGE 159, 223 <298 para. 169> with further references;
161, 163 <269 para. 291> – Economic costs of
child-raising; 163, 107 <138 para. 86>; 167, 163 <212
para. 115>). The legislative purpose is typically derived from the
objective intent of the legislator (cf. BVerfGE 150, 244 <276 para.
74>; 161, 63 <93 para. 57> – Wind farm participatory
companies; 167, 163 <212 para. 115> – Contergan II)
and is to be determined by recognised methods of legal interpretation,
i.e. based on the wording, the legislative materials and legislative
history, the systematic approach and the spirit and purpose. These
methods are not mutually exclusive, but instead complement one another
(cf. BVerfGE 144, 20 <212 f. para. 555>; 161, 63 <93 para.
57>). Based thereon, purposes that must be taken into account in
constitutional review are in particular – but not exclusively – those
that are plausible based on the legislator’s intent or that have been
named by the bodies involved in the legislative process during the
proceedings before the Federal Constitutional Court (cf. BVerfGE 112,
226 <244>; 120, 82 <115>; 140, 65 <79 f. para. 33>;
163, 107 <139 para. 87>). By contrast, unnamed purposes or
purposes that only came into play after the law was adopted cannot be
taken into account where such purposes would fail to meet or would
distort essential aspects of the clear legislative objective (BVerfGE
167, 163 <212 para. 115>).

bb) Based on these standards, the age limit in § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries pursues
legitimate purposes. The age limit serves to achieve an appropriate age
structure within the profession of notary in the interest of the proper
functioning of the administration of justice (see (1) below), a fair
distribution of career opportunities among generations (see (2) below),
and the protection of the administration of justice from risks that may
arise from age-related facultative decline among notaries (see (3)
below). By contrast, the age limit does not serve to facilitate
personnel planning by the Land justice departments for lawyers
commissioned as notaries (see (4) below).

(1) The determination of a maximum age for practising as notary
serves to guarantee the proper functioning of the administration of
justice. The explanatory memorandum to the recommendation of the
Committee for Legal Affairs focuses on the objective of ensuring an
appropriate age structure within the profession of notary (cf. BTDrucks
11/8307, p. 18). However, this in itself is not the purpose of the law.
Rather, it is a means for ensuring the proper functioning of the
administration of justice. Clients should be able to choose from
notaries of different ages who have a minimum level of professional
experience gained through the number and types of notarial transactions
conducted by them in all areas of notarial practice. By contrast, an
ageing profession would mean that clients could increasingly only engage
older notaries (cf. Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the Second
Chamber of the First Senate of 29 October 1992 - 1 BvR 1581/91 -, juris,
para. 7). This would jeopardise the proper functioning of the
administration of justice in two respects. First, the successors of
retired notaries would have little professional experience because of
their later admission to the profession. Second, it would be difficult
for clients to engage a notary of their preferred age group, even if
their personal trust in the notary might be based precisely on that
notary belonging to a certain age group.

The age limit is thus functionally linked to further age-related
provisions inserted into the law by the 1991 amendment, namely the age
structure clause under § 4(2) of the Federal Code for Notaries and the
maximum age for the initial appointment as notary (today § 5(4) Federal
Code for Notaries). These provisions are also aimed at achieving an
appropriate age structure among notaries, as set out in the wording of
§ 4 second sentence of the Federal Code for Notaries, and addressing the
risks of an ageing profession. Guaranteeing the proper functioning of
the administration of justice in the sense of ensuring both the greatest
possible diversity and a minimum of professional experience among
notaries is a legitimate legislative aim.

(2) The age limit of 70 years also aims to achieve a fair
distribution of career opportunities among generations (cf. Federal
Constitutional Court, Order of the Second Chamber of the First Senate of
5 January 2011 - 1 BvR 2870/10 -, juris, para. 13). According to the
legislative materials on the 1991 amendment, the legislator sought to
enact stricter rules for entering the profession, while at the same time
keeping the profession open for younger people (BTDrucks 11/8307, p.
17). This constitutes a legitimate purpose for employment and social
policy.

(3) The age limit in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the
Federal Code for Notaries also serves the legitimate purpose of
protecting the administration of justice from risks that may arise from
age-related facultative decline among notaries.

While this aim cannot be ascertained from the legislative materials,
it constitutes an objective purpose in principle. It is based on the
assumption that a person’s faculties deteriorate as they age. The
ability of older officeholders to properly practise their occupation may
therefore be impaired, jeopardising legal interests that are affected by
their work. The Federal Constitutional Court has held in several cases
that the prevention of such risks was a legitimate purpose of statutory
or collective age limits for the practice of an occupation (cf. BVerfGE
9, 338 <345> – Midwives; 64, 72 <83> – Test
engineers; Chamber Decisions of the Federal Constitutional Court,
Kammerentscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGK 4,
219 <221 f.> – Airline pilots; 10, 227 <233> –
Airline pilots; Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the
Second Chamber of the First Senate of 31 March 1998 - 1 BvR 2167/93
inter alia -, juris, para. 30 – Doctors).

This purpose is not contradicted, in terms of the systematic
approach, by the fact that the age limit pursuant to § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries does not apply to
deputy notaries within the meaning of § 39 of the Federal Code for
Notaries or to administrators for notaries (Notariatsverwalter)
within the meaning of § 56 of the Federal Code for Notaries (cf. Federal
Court of Justice, Order of 31 July 2000 […]). This is because the law
entails lower performance requirements for these tasks than for the
office of notary as such. Deputy notaries only stand in for absent
notaries (cf. § 39 Federal Code for Notaries). Administrators for
notaries only temporarily exercise the office of notary and typically
only liquidate ongoing notarial business (cf. § 56(1) and (2) Federal
Code for Notaries). Therefore, the prerequisites for being appointed as
deputy notary or as administrator are generally lower than those for
being appointed as notary (cf. § 39(3), § 56(6) Federal Code for
Notaries).

This purpose is also not contradicted by the fact that the Federal
Code for Notaries also contains a legal mechanism by which the
administration of justice can be protected in individual cases of
notaries who can no longer fulfil the requirements of office. It is true
that a notary can be removed from office pursuant to § 50(1) no. 7 of
the Federal Code for Notaries if they are unable to properly exercise
their duties for health reasons. This also includes the inability to
exercise notarial duties due to age-related general weakness of body or
mind ([…]); a notary can also be temporarily suspended for these reasons
(cf. § 54 Federal Code for Notaries). However, in systematic terms, such
an exclusion based on specific grounds does not rule out a
further-reaching rule based on typification such as an age limit.

The purpose does not lose its legitimacy because it risks
stereotyping by age by reinforcing the view that older people are
generally seen to perform less well in physical and cognitive terms (cf.
in this regard the statement of the Federal Anti-Discrimination Agency,
p. 10 ff.). The fact that ageing is an individual process (cf. para. 129
ff.) does not call into question the legitimacy of the purpose as such,
at most, it may call into question the suitability, necessity and
reasonableness of the age limit.

(4) By contrast, the age limit does not serve to facilitate personnel
planning by the Land justice departments for lawyers
commissioned as notaries, despite the assertions in several statements
submitted in the present proceedings, which assume that the age limit
provides the justice departments with information as to when each notary
will leave office at the latest. According to the statements, this makes
it easier to fill vacant notary positions in a timely manner. However,
such purpose cannot be derived from either the wording of the Federal
Code for Notaries or from the legislative materials. It is also not
objectively ascertainable that there is a need for facilitated personnel
planning. In the case of lawyers commissioned as notaries, the
activities of the justice departments are essentially limited to
determining the need for the appointment of notaries at regular
intervals pursuant to § 4 of the Federal Code for Notaries and, when
necessary, to advertise open notary positions. This is necessary
regardless of whether an age limit is in place. The situation for
lawyers commissioned as notaries thus differs from the situation of
those serving as notaries as their primary occupation, because in the
latter case, the number of positions for trainee notaries (cf. § 5a and
§ 7 Federal Code for Notaries) must be based on the number of notary
positions that will later become vacant; it also differs from the
situation in the public service.

b) Despite changing factual circumstances, the age limit pursuant to
§ 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries
remains suitable under constitutional law to achieve the legislative
purposes. The suitability requirement is satisfied if there is a
possibility of achieving the legislative purpose with the statutory
provisions. A provision can only be found to be unsuitable if it cannot
further the legislative purpose in any way or even counteracts this
purpose (BVerfGE 158, 282 <336 para. 131>; 163, 107 <149 para.
111>; established case-law).

aa) The age limit helps maintain an appropriate age structure in the
interest of the proper functioning of the administration of justice and
a fair distribution of career opportunities among generations (cf. para.
114).

(1) The statutory age limit of 70 years is based on the fact that new
notaries can only enter the profession when and insofar as a sufficient
number of notary positions becomes vacant. This is because the number of
positions is fixed by law in order to prevent the development of
notarial offices that are not economically viable (cf. § 4 Federal Code
for Notaries). The age limit accelerates the creation of vacancies by
continuously forcing older notaries to retire and limiting the length of
time they can remain in the profession. It can thus serve to open up the
profession to younger people and thereby rejuvenate it.

(2) Changing factual circumstances, namely the shortage of applicants
for the position of lawyer commissioned as notary that has arisen in
recent years, mean that the age limit is only constitutionally suitable
to a limited extent, but these changes do not render it completely
unsuitable.

In introducing the age limit in 1991, the legislator was acting in
response to considerable increases in the number of licensed lawyers.
Between 1980 and 1990, the number of lawyers had risen from 36,077 to
56,638. By 2000, it had risen to 104,067. In light of the oversupply of
candidates for the position of lawyer commissioned as notary across
Germany, the age limit significantly improved younger candidates’
prospects of entering the profession. For each notary who left the
profession due to the age limit, a younger notary could start to
practise. Consequently, in order to be able to enter the profession,
potential young notaries had to rely on older notaries leaving the
profession when they turned 70.

This situation has changed profoundly. The overall number of lawyers
is stagnating, and the number of lawyers in private practice who are not
licensed as an in-house lawyer or have a double license, i.e. those who
could be commissioned as notaries, is in substantial decline. In most
regions, there is a permanent shortage of candidates for the position of
lawyer commissioned as notary (cf. para. 34 ff.).

Nevertheless, the age limit for lawyers commissioned as notaries has
not completely ceased to have the effect originally intended by the
legislator. Rather, there are clear regional differences. In the Local
Court districts affected by the shortage of applicants, lawyers
commissioned as notaries leave office upon reaching the age limit, but
there is an insufficient number of younger lawyers to take their place.
A considerable proportion of open positions remains vacant. Thus, in
terms of the economic viability of their notarial practice, those
entering the profession are not reliant on positions becoming vacant as
a result of the age limit (in more detail para. 170 ff.). In these
regions, the age limit thus fails to achieve its purpose – creating
vacancies to achieve an appropriate age structure in the interest of the
proper functioning of the administration of justice and a fair
distribution of career opportunities among generations. On the other
hand, there are a few Local Court districts, mostly in big cities, that
have a surplus of applicants. In these districts, the age limit
continues to help achieve the legislative purposes of maintaining the
proper functioning of the administration of justice and creating career
opportunities for younger notaries. This residual ability of the age
limit to achieve its purpose is sufficient for it to meet the
constitutional criterion of suitability.

bb) The age limit of 70 years is also suitable for protecting the
administration of justice from risks that may arise due to age-related
facultative decline among notaries. Current knowledge on how age affects
professional faculties does not completely rule out the suitability of
the age limit. According to the statements submitted in the proceedings
and the discussions in the oral hearing, the situation is as
follows:

(1) According to the assessment in the statements of both the German
Association for Gerontology and Geriatrics and the German Centre for
Age-Related Questions, empirical studies have shown that the cognitive
ageing process is highly individualised. There are therefore no
overarching correlations between higher age and professional faculties.
This does not apply to work that relies on high processing speed, such
as that of airline pilots.

As people age, cognitive skills that are based on rapid processing of
information, such as the quick identification of dangerous traffic
situations as well as decision-making and action based thereon
(so-called fluid intelligence […]), decline; these declines may begin as
early as one’s 30s. By contrast, other cognitive skills are less
susceptible to age-related decline and can compensate for the lower
processing speed. So-called crystallised intelligence is based on
acquired knowledge and comprises skills and abilities based on
experience and stored in long-term memory. Studies have shown that
crystallised intelligence tends to increase into old age or at least
remain stable, as the German Centre for Age-Related Questions has
submitted. This notwithstanding, according to the German Association for
Gerontology and Geriatrics, in view of the risk of dementia, there are
serious doubts as to the cognitive abilities to practise as notary for
approximately one-eighth to one-sixth of those over 70. Although this is
put into perspective by the fact that this assessment also includes
people with only mild cognitive impairments, it is still not entirely
negligible.

(2) In light of these scientific findings, the age limit still meets
the constitutional criterion of suitability. It is true that the age
limit is a crude instrument which, to a considerable extent, affects
persons in respect of whom there is no indication that their
professional faculties are limited. From a gerontological perspective,
rigid age limits are therefore not recommended. Nevertheless, the
provision can further the legislative purpose by also including notaries
who, once they have turned 70, can no longer fulfil the requirements of
office due to ageing.

c) The age limit of 70 years is also constitutionally necessary to
achieve the purposes pursued.

Interferences with fundamental rights must not go further than is
necessary to protect the common good. A measure falls short of this
standard if an equally effective means is available that would be less
intrusive for the affected fundamental right holders and would not
entail greater burdens for third parties or the general public. It must
be clearly established that the alternative measure is equally effective
in every respect for achieving the purpose pursued. A less intrusive
means in this sense is not available in the present case.

aa) A higher age limit – for example an age limit of 75 or 80 – does
not constitute a less intrusive means to achieve the purposes pursued.
While such an age limit would amount to less serious interference with
occupational freedom, it would not be equally effective with regard to
all three purposes. Older lawyers commissioned as notaries would leave
office at a later time, which would mean a decrease in the number of
vacant positions available for younger notaries, at least in regions
where there is still a surplus of applicants. Likewise, fewer notaries
who are no longer able to fulfil the requirements of office due to
ageing would be affected by the age limit.

bb) The same considerations would apply as a starting point if the
age limit were not raised generally and instead continued practice for
notaries over 70 were contingent on continued ability to fulfil the
requirements of office. Such a provision would also lead to less severe
interference, because the expiry of notarial office in case of a loss of
faculties is less severe than an absolute obligation to leave office as
a result of a rigid age limit. This assessment of the severity of
interference is not refuted by the fact that the ability to fulfil the
requirements of office would have to be reviewed at regular
intervals.

However, a review of such ability in individual cases would not be
equally effective with regard to the purposes of achieving an
appropriate age structure in the interest of the proper functioning of
the administration of justice and a fair distribution of career
opportunities among generations. Based on the statements submitted by
the German Centre for Age-Related Questions and the German Association
for Gerontology and Geriatrics, the proportion of notaries over 70 who
would be unable to fulfil the requirements of office due to ageing would
be rather low. Thus, such a provision – similar to measures pursuant to
§ 50(1) no. 7 of the Federal Code for Notaries – would probably only
result in a relatively small number of notaries leaving office. This
would result in fewer vacant positions compared to the current rigid age
limit.

It is true that such a provision could provide more targeted
protection from risks that may arise from age-related decline in
faculties among notaries. However, it would impose additional financial
burdens on the general public, because regular performance reviews for
older lawyers commissioned as notaries would likely require significant
administrative efforts.

cc) A restriction of the age limit to those Local Court districts in
which a surplus of applicants exists could be based on a provision under
social law – now repealed – that provided for regional exemptions from
age limits for doctors based on need (cf. § 95(7) of the Fifth Book of
the Code of Social Law, Fünftes Buch Sozialgesetzbuch – SGB V
in the version of 22 December 2006, BGBl I p. 3439). However, this does
not constitute an equally effective means that would be less intrusive
for the affected fundamental right holders and would not entail greater
burdens for third parties or the general public.

It is true that the severity of interference would be lower in that a
smaller number of persons would be affected because the age limit would
only continue to apply in a few regions. However, this would mean that
the legislative purpose of protection from the risks arising from
age-related decline in faculties among notaries could only be realised
to a limited extent unless an obligation to subject to performance
reviews was also introduced. Such provision would be equally effective
with regard to the purposes of achieving an appropriate age structure in
the interest of the proper functioning of the administration of justice
and a fair distribution of career opportunities among generations. Yet
it is within the margin of appreciation of the legislator to consider
that such a provision would entail greater burdens for notaries, clients
and departments of justice. This is because the relationship between the
number of applicants and vacancies can vary greatly from region to
region, even in times of an almost nationwide shortage of applicants.
This would make it difficult to set long-term and reliable regionally
differentiated age limits. It would entail considerable uncertainties
for the career prospects of older lawyers commissioned as notaries and
would significantly complicate their planning – for example with regard
to their membership in law firms. Clients, too, would face uncertainties
if they engaged older notaries whose practice depended on unclear
localised rules. Finally, regularly reassessing the need for notaries in
a way that ensures legal certainty in each individual Local Court
district would require significant administrative efforts.

dd) Facilitating access to the profession of lawyer commissioned as
notary by lowering the requirements for entering the profession in order
to increase the number of applicants, as was proposed in some of the
statements and in the oral hearing, also does not constitute a less
intrusive means under constitutional law.

This would include amendments to the Federal Code for Notaries to
broaden the appeal and increase the economic viability of the profession
of lawyer commissioned as notary. For example, the time limits for
previous practice as a lawyer or the restriction to specific
jurisdictions, set out respectively in § 5b(1) nos. 1 and 2 of the
Federal Code for Notaries, could be abolished or made less restrictive.
The notarial examination pursuant to § 5b(1) no. 3 of the Federal Code
for Notaries, the further training obligation after the examination and
the practical training pursuant to § 5b(4) of the Federal Code for
Notaries could also be abolished, simplified or made less restrictive.
It would also be possible to combine several of these measures.

While such reforms would be less intrusive for fundamental right
holders than the age limit, they could not, from the outset, achieve the
purpose of protecting the administration of justice from risks that may
arise from age-related decline in faculties among notaries. There is no
need to decide whether these reforms could achieve the other purposes –
maintaining an appropriate age structure in the interest of the
functioning of the administration of justice and a fair distribution of
career opportunities among generations – in the same way as the age
limit. This is because making it easier to enter the profession would
probably entail burdens for clients in the form of the deterioration of
notarial services. Ensuring high-quality notarial services is also
within the margin of appreciation of the legislator. The legislator has
made use of this margin by integrating the age limit into an overall
concept that aims to ensure high-quality notarial services. As early as
in 1991, with the Act Amending the Professional Regulations for Notaries
that introduced the age limit, the legislator restricted access to the
profession of lawyer commissioned as notary to ensure high quality
within the profession (cf. BTDrucks 11/6007, pp. 1, 9). The 2009 Act
Amending the Federal Code for Notaries (BGBl I p. 696) made the notarial
examination a prerequisite for appointment as notary in order to
guarantee certain minimum standards (cf. BTDrucks 16/4972, p. 1). When
the legislator adopted the Act Modernising the Professional Regulations
for Notaries of 25 June 2021 (BGBl I p. 2154), it was known that certain
regions were faced with a shortage of applicants (cf. BTDrucks 19/26828,
pp. 113 f.). Nevertheless, the legislator generally refrained from
making it easier to enter the profession. It merely modified the
requirement of a local waiting period with the newly inserted § 5b(3) of
the Federal Code for Notaries. Instead, the legislator took other
measures to broaden the appeal of the profession of notary, such as the
possibility to temporarily resign from office for family reasons – in
particular to care for children or to provide nursing care (§ 48b
Federal Code for Notaries). The constitutional criterion of necessity
does not require the legislator to depart from these quality
standards.

ee) Finally, raising the fees for notarial services is also not a
less intrusive means under constitutional law. While raising fees could
make working as a notary more attractive and thus further the interest
of younger candidates, it would impose additional financial burdens on
clients. This could make access to the administration of justice more
difficult and would thus run counter to the public interest. Moreover,
such a measure would not be equally effective in providing protection
from risks that may arise from age-related decline in faculties among
notaries.

d) The age limit of 70 pursuant to § 47 no. 2 first alternative and
§ 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries is no longer proportionate in the
strict sense with regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries. It places
an unreasonable burden on the fundamental right holders.

For a measure to meet the constitutional criterion of
appropriateness, and thus proportionality in the strict sense, the
purpose pursued by the measure, and its likelihood of achieving that
purpose, may not be disproportionate to the severity of the interference
(cf. BVerfGE 155, 119 <178 para. 128> – Subscriber data
II; 161, 299 <384 para. 203> – Proof of vaccination
<COVID-19>; 166, 1 <71>; established case-law). In an
overall assessment balancing the severity of the burdens against the
importance and urgency of the reasons invoked to justify them, the
limits of what is reasonable must be observed (cf. BVerfGE 152, 68
<137 para. 183> – Sanctions imposed under social law). It
is for the legislator to strike a balance between the extent and
severity of the interference with fundamental rights, on the one hand,
and the provision’s importance for achieving legitimate purposes on the
other (cf. BVerfGE 159, 355 <413>; 166, 1 <71 f. para.
155>). The prohibition of excessive measures (Übermaßverbot)
requires that the more severely individual freedom is restricted, the
more significant the pursued interests of the common good must be. In
qualitative terms, the severity of interference is determined by the
extent to which the actions and legal positions protected by fundamental
rights, including the associated economic consequences, are curtailed
(cf. Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the First Senate of 24
October 2024 - 1 BvL 10/20 - Law on names; adult adoption,
para. 64; BVerfGE 166, 1 <65 para. 144> – Child marriages
– in relation to the criterion of necessity).

In order to correctly assess the severity of interference, the
different protective dimensions of Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law must be
taken into account. Occupational freedom entails both an economic
dimension and a dimension related to the development of one’s
personality (cf. para. 103). Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law thus gives
definition to the fundamental right to the free development of one’s
personality in the area of individual achievement and creating and
earning a livelihood and aims to have the least amount of regulation of
occupational activity possible (cf. BVerfGE 82, 209 <223>; 163,
107 <134 para. 73>).

Based on these standards, the rule imposed by § 47 no. 2 first
alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries is no longer
proportionate in the strict sense, to the extent it applies to lawyers
commissioned as notaries. The age limit of 70 years results in serious
interference with the fundamental right to occupational freedom of
lawyers commissioned as notaries (see aa) below). This must be weighed
against interests of the common good, which also have considerable
weight (see bb) below). However, given that the degree to which the age
limit achieves its purposes has decreased over time (see cc) below), the
outcome of the weighing of interests is no longer constitutionally
appropriate (see dd) below).

aa) The interference with occupational freedom is serious, even when
attenuating factors are taken into account.

(1) The intensity of the interference is considerable. The age limit
is a rule that affects the choice of occupation (cf. BVerfGE 7, 377
<406>). Upon reaching this limit, the commission as notary
expires. As the decisive criterion of age is impossible to avoid, the
fundamental right holders have no influence on the end of their career
nor can they change it to suit their own circumstances. The law does not
provide for exemptions from the age limit or a gradual transition to
retirement. The interference equally affects both areas of protection
guaranteed by occupational freedom, i.e. obtaining an economic
livelihood and developing one’s personality (cf. para. 103).

(2) The interference is mitigated to some extent by the possibility
for those that must forfeit their office due to the age limit to remain
active in the same field as a deputy or administrator or to resume their
primary occupation as a lawyer. Nevertheless, the degree of mitigation
is limited.

The practice as deputy or administrator is not remotely the same as
the regular activities of a notary. Deputy notaries only carry out
duties in the absence of the notary (§ 39(1) Federal Code for Notaries);
notarial administration is a temporary position from the outset that
mainly concerns liquidation activities (§ 56 Federal Code for Notaries,
cf. also para. 117). These functions thus lack the specific qualities of
a liberal profession with corresponding latitude. Moreover, a notary who
had to forfeit their office can only practise as a deputy or
administrator if there is a need for such services and they are
appointed to the position. They have no control over this, and there is
no right to being appointed.

The possibility of continuing to practise as a lawyer does not
mitigate the interference with the right to practise as a notary in
order to freely develop one’s personality. The severity of interference
may be mitigated when it comes to securing one’s economic livelihood.
However, this only applies insofar as a lawyer commissioned as notary
who had to forfeit their office of notary has actual prospects as a
lawyer.

The loss of economic livelihood incurred through the expiry of the
commission as notary is also compensated by pension entitlements in
part. Lawyers commissioned as notaries are compulsory members of a
lawyers’ pension fund (cf., e.g., § 2 of the Act on Pensions for Lawyers
in North Rhine-Westphalia, Gesetz über die Rechtsanwaltsversorgung
Nordrhein-Westfalen). Moreover, they can prepare for the age limit
and make private provision (cf. Federal Constitutional Court, Order of
the Second Chamber of the First Senate of 29 October 1992 - 1 BvR
1581/91 -, juris, para. 10). However, this only mitigates the severity
of interference to a limited extent, since it does not affect the
protection afforded to the free development of one’s personality and
pension entitlements are typically lower than the income of active
notaries.

(3) Seventy years is a substantially later age than the standard
retirement age set by law at which civil servants and employees may
claim full retirement benefits. On its own, however, this does not
result in a mitigation of the severity of the interference. While the
Federal Association of Notaries claimed in its statement that this
leaves notaries a sufficiently long time period to practise their
occupation and realise ‘economic and other benefits’, this consideration
has not been supported by facts.

Lawyers commissioned as notaries – much like professionals in higher
positions in the civil service and other occupations requiring higher
education – generally begin this occupation at a relatively late age.
The reason for this is that § 5b(1) no. 1 of the Federal Code for
Notaries provides for a waiting period of five years. Thus, only
approximately 5% of lawyers commissioned as notaries are under 40 when
they are appointed (cf. para. 31).

In addition, in looking at society as a whole, career and employment
paths have become more flexible and interruptions in the standard
progression from education to a career to retirement have become
increasingly more common. This is reflected, for example, in the
increase in part-time work and more frequent interruptions of employment
by child-raising periods (cf. Federal Statistical Office […]). The
number of older workers has also increased significantly in recent
years. While in 2013 50% of 60 to 64-year-olds were in employment, that
proportion rose to 65% in 2023. Among 65 to 69-year-olds, it rose from
13% to 20%. Self-employment becomes ever more common as people age. In
2023, 31% of over-65-year-olds in employment were self-employed. This
proportion was many times higher than the average for all persons in
employment (9%) (cf. Federal Statistical Office […]). These developments
are driven by demographic changes that can also be traced back to higher
life expectancies and improved performance of older people, as
emphasised by the German Association for Gerontology and Geriatrics in
the present proceedings.

Moreover, the perception of age in society has changed and become
more differentiated, as pointed out by the Federal Anti-Discrimination
Agency. Previous perceptions of age, which generally associated old age
with deteriorating faculties, little openness to change, or illness, are
increasingly replaced by perceptions of age highlighting the diversity
and individuality of life in old age (cf. […] BTDrucks 17/3815).

The typically late entry into the profession of lawyer commissioned
as notary, the increasingly individualised progression of career and
employment paths and changing perceptions of age argue against the
normative determination of an age after which an interference with
occupational freedom is less serious.

(4) Contrary to the view stated in the legal opinion submitted by the
Federal Association of Notaries, a notary’s income does not merit less
protection than income from private commercial activities, and the
interference is not rendered less serious on this account. It is true
that the fees levied by notaries on the basis of the Act on Court and
Notary Fees (Gesetz über Kosten der freiwilligen Gerichtsbarkeit für
Gerichte und Notare – GNotKG) do not constitute remuneration for
services rendered, but are paid on the basis of the principle of cost
recovery and equivalence instead. For reasons of the common good,
transactions of a lower monetary value are subsidised by transactions of
higher value ([…]). However, the fact that notarial activities are
guided by the common good does not mean that the income generated
thereby merits less protection under constitutional law. Notaries, who
by law may only generate income through fees, do not merit less
protection in this respect than members of other occupations.

bb) The interference with occupational freedom must be weighed
against protected interests of the common good of considerable weight
that are pursued by the age limit in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and
§ 48a of the Federal Code for Notaries.

(1) The proper functioning of the administration of justice – namely,
providing high-quality notarial services (§§ 20 through 24 Federal Code
for Notaries) carried out by able, sufficiently experienced notaries of
various ages – is of great importance both on an individual level and
for society as a whole. This follows from the fundamental legislative
decisions on substantive law and the administration of justice, because
the legislator requires certain legal transactions and legal acts to be
certified by a notary. Due to the formal requirements of substantive
law, property transactions in particular are reliant on certification by
notaries (cf. §§ 311b and 873 Civil Code, Bürgerliches
Gesetzbuch – BGB). Similarly, several other legal transactions in
contract, inheritance and family law and in corporate law are subject to
formal requirements requiring the involvement of a notary (cf., e.g.,
§§ 518, 1410, 2276 Civil Code, § 2 Act on Limited Liability Companies,
Gesetz betreffend die Gesellschaften mit beschränkter Haftung –
GmbHG; §§ 23, 30, 33, 37, 130, 181, 235, 248 Stock Corporation Act,
Aktiengesetz – AktG). The interests at stake for the persons
concerned are typically also protected by fundamental rights, namely by
Art. 6(1), Art. 9(1), Art. 12(1) and Art. 14(1) of the Basic Law. On the
other hand, with regard to the application of the law, the legislator
has largely removed this aspect of the administration of justice from
direct oversight by the state and entrusted it exclusively to notaries
as independent holders of a public office (§ 1 Federal Code for
Notaries). Since persons concerned generally have no equally suitable
alternatives to formalised legal transactions, nor to their
certification by a notary, they are reliant on the proper functioning of
the notarial system in many areas of the exercise of fundamental rights
that are significant in personal and economic terms. It is therefore not
only critical that notarial services be offered, these must also be
high-quality services provided by able notaries.

(2) Equally important is the social and employment policy objective
of the legislator to provide a fair distribution of career opportunities
among generations. This purpose also serves to give effect to the rights
of potential notaries under Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law to enter the
profession.

cc) With regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries, however, the age
limit provides only a very limited contribution towards these
objectives.

(1) This applies in the first instance to maintaining an appropriate
age structure in the interest of the proper functioning of the
administration of justice and a fair distribution of career
opportunities among generations. While the age limit was effective in
