achieving these objectives at the time of its introduction, today this
is only the case to a considerably more limited degree.

(a) By introducing the age limit, the legislator intended to free up
notarial positions for younger people and open up career opportunities.
For lawyers commissioned as notaries, however, this intended purpose has
become meaningless in those regions in which there are too few
applicants for such positions. Any applicant that meets the statutory
requirements can obtain a position in these regions. This is now the
case for the majority of Higher Regional Court districts in which
lawyers may be commissioned as notaries (cf. para. 34 ff.).

(b) The diminished effectiveness of the age limit also cannot be
remedied by any alternative means of implementing this measure.

In the initial proceedings, the Federal Court of Justice assumed that
the function of the age limit could still be fulfilled even with an
insufficient number of applicants by having younger lawyers commissioned
as notaries take over the transactions and revenues previously handled
by their older counterparts who leave the profession. The court argued
that it is only when older notaries leave the profession that younger
notaries would have sufficient prospects of an appropriate number of
transactions to generate sufficient revenues, and thus be willing to
take on the considerable burdens associated with entering the profession
(cf. para. 42). The Federal Government and several expert third parties
endorsed this view in their statements. This is based on the assessment
that potential candidates for notarial office are deterred from seeking
a commission as a notary by the significant financial burden and time
required to pass the notarial examination and by the high costs for
establishing an office. This obstacle could be overcome through
considerable economic incentives.

The need for such incentives appears doubtful, at least with regard
to those regions in which lawyers are commissioned as notaries and a
great number of notarial positions are vacant due to lack of applicants.
There is no empirical evidence that the ‘freeing up’ of revenues
provides any meaningful incentive to apply for a commission as a notary.
The widespread vacancies that already exist suggest that the incentives
would be minimal at best. Furthermore, the view that the aforementioned
incentives are necessary is based on the assumption that the shortage of
applicants for a commission as a notary does not have demographic
causes, but is a structural problem only. However, this assumption is
not sufficiently plausible. Rather, according to the Institute for the
Legal Profession at the University of Cologne, several interrelated
factors are responsible for the dwindling number of applicants.

(aa) One factor is the demographics of the profession of lawyer as
the prerequisite for the commission as a notary, because it reduces the
number of potential lawyers who can be commissioned as notaries.

While the number of licensed lawyers has remained relatively stable
since 2015, with annual increases or decreases of less than one percent,
only lawyers in private practice who are not licensed as in-house
lawyers or have a double license can be commissioned as notaries. This
is because in-house lawyers typically do not meet the requirement of
§ 5b(1) no. 1 of the Federal Code for Notaries, according to which only
lawyers who have worked for various clients on a not insignificant scale
can be commissioned as notaries (cf. Federal Court of Justice, Judgment
of 23 November 2015 - NotZ (Brfg) 2/15 -, Decisions of the Federal Court
of Justice in Civil Matters, Entscheidungen des Bundesgerichtshofes
in Zivilsachen – BGHZ 208, 39 <45 para. 15>). According to
information provided by the Institute for the Legal Profession, lawyers
with a double license typically do not perform much work as practising
lawyers in addition to their work as in-house lawyers for their employer
and therefore also do not meet the aforementioned requirement. In
addition, the proportion of lawyers who work in private practice has
decreased considerably due to the considerable reduction in the number
of new licenses and due to the increasing number of younger lawyers who
completely forego their license after a few years in the profession or
become in-house lawyers. As of 1 January 2017, the number of lawyers in
private practice without a license as in-house lawyer or a double
license was 60,456 in the regions where lawyers are commissioned as
notaries; by 1 January 2025, this number had fallen to 54,270. The
decline over this period was below average (under 5%) in big cities,
such as in the districts overseen by the Frankfurt/Main and the Berlin
Higher Regional Courts and above average (13% to 14.7%) in the districts
overseen by the Celle, Hamm, Schleswig, Bremen and Brunswick Higher
Regional Courts.

According to the plausible and uncontested calculations of the
Institute for the Legal Profession, the number of new lawyers entering
the profession each year, and therefore also potential applicants for a
commission as a notary, is only around 700 to 900. Given that only a
fraction of lawyers even considers becoming a notary, according to
information provided by the Institute for the Legal Profession based on
empirical studies, this number is insufficient to replace the notaries
leaving office.

(bb) Furthermore, young lawyers have changing career preferences.
According to studies regarding the career paths of young lawyers, 30% of
lawyers with six years of professional experience had their own practice
or co-owned a practice in 2012, whereas by 2024 this number had fallen
to 11%. Substantially fewer salaried lawyers have the intention of
ultimately establishing their own businesses and, in particular, there
is little interest in being commissioned as a notary. According to a
survey among younger lawyers from 2023/24, only 18% wish to be
commissioned as a notary at some point. The reasons given for this
result were lack of interest in the work (66% of respondents), the
considerable time required to qualify (39%), and, to a lesser extent,
the slim chance of obtaining a notarial office (34%), the high costs of
qualification (16%) and the lack of economic attractiveness (4%). This
is in line with the considerable decline in the number of persons who
have taken the notarial examination, which was communicated by the
Institute for the Legal Profession.

Based on this data on demographic developments and career
preferences, the shortage of applicants for a commission as notary has
multiple causes. While the age limit may have certain effects in
increasing the local attractiveness of notarial positions as
transactions and revenues are ‘freed up’, these effects are limited,
given that such greater attractiveness cannot influence demographics nor
override all personal preferences; the lack of economic attractiveness
also is not, according to the aforementioned studies, one of the main
reasons for not seeking a commission as a notary. Furthermore, in many
regions where lawyers are commissioned as notaries, there is such a
large number of vacancies even today that sufficient revenues would be
available, which means that the creation of more vacancies through the
age limit does not offer additional career prospects.

(c) The evident ineffectiveness of the age limit in achieving the
legislative purposes is established and is not projected to change.

Based on available data, a significant shortage of applicants can be
identified as early as 2012 (cf. para. 34 ff.). There are no factual
indications that this situation will improve in the future, as the
determinative demographic factors and career preferences will continue
to develop in the same direction. According to forecasts by the
Institute for the Legal Profession at the University of Cologne, an
increase in the number of new licenses for lawyers cannot be expected in
the medium and long term. While the number of graduates with a law
degree is expected to rise over the next few years, there will be a huge
decline in the number of graduates by 2040, which will be accompanied by
a great need to replace lawyers born between 1968 to 1975, who will be
entering retirement. Therefore, it must be expected that the demographic
factors will become even stronger. The trend of young lawyers not
establishing their own businesses will, according to the assessment of
the Institute for the Legal Profession, likely become even more
pronounced in the future. The Institute emphasises the increasing share
of women in particular, who are statistically more likely to prefer
salaried employment than men.

An assessment to the contrary is not justified with reference to the
developments in the revenues earned by lawyers commissioned as notaries.
It was asserted in the present proceedings that the number of notarial
transactions is expected to decline. Given the mechanisms of the needs
assessment pursuant to § 4 of the Federal Code for Notaries, this would
lead to fewer notarial positions being created (cf. para. 14). Under
these circumstances, the age limit could once again achieve its purposes
to a greater degree. However, there are no discernible factual
indications of a sustained reversal of the current situation. According
to the data available, the annual number of notarial transactions is
subject to regular fluctuations. The overall number of certifications
conducted by lawyers commissioned as notaries amounted to between
approximately 2.8 to 2.9 million every year from 2015 to 2022. In 2023,
it decreased to 2.5 million, however, according to the Federal
Association of Notaries, this was based on the generally weak economy
rather than on lasting factors.

Adjusting the calculation of the number of appointments necessary for
a given region to reduce the number of positions advertised, as the
Federal Association of Notaries has proposed and some Land
justice departments have considered or even already implemented, would
not improve the effectiveness of the age limit in achieving the
legislative purposes. Such a measure is not able to halt the decline in
the number of applicants, which is linked to demographic factors.
Moreover, the number of younger lawyers who are commissioned as notaries
and take the place of older notaries is insufficient. Thus, adjusting
the calculation of the number of appointments necessary does not lead to
the appointment of a greater number of notaries nor to younger notaries,
at least not to a notable degree. What is more, the legislator’s leeway
for raising the number of appointments is limited from the outset, given
that it must be ensured, pursuant to § 4 second sentence of the Federal
Code for Notaries, that clients are adequately supplied with notarial
services. This includes ensuring that notaries are available close to
someone’s place of residence or business (cf. Federal Court of Justice,
Order of 20 July 2020 - NotZ (Brfg) 5/19 -, para. 13 with further
references), which requires a certain density of notarial practices,
particularly in more sparsely populated rural regions. Moreover, it must
be ensured that lawyers commissioned as notaries can practise as
notaries as their secondary occupation (cf. § 3(2) Federal Code for
Notaries). It would be impermissible under ordinary law for the justice
departments to take measures that would lead to this type of notary
resembling notaries who exercise the office as their primary
occupation.

(2) The legislative purpose of protecting the administration of
justice from risks that may arise from age-related decline in faculties
among notaries is also only served to a limited degree by the age limit
in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for
Notaries.

In the statements submitted by the German Centre for Age-Related
Questions and the German Society for Gerontology and Geriatrics, it was
consistently emphasised that the cognitive ageing process is strongly
influenced by individual factors and that, in the notarial profession,
there are no overarching correlations between increased age and
professional faculties (cf. para. 129 ff.). In this respect, there is a
difference between the notarial profession and other professions which
involve the rapid cognitive processing of information, such as that of
an airline pilot.

The age limit is not justified under these circumstances, in which
all officeholders must forfeit their positions upon reaching age 70
without regard to their individual abilities. While it can be assumed,
based on the empirical evidence, that the age limit also concerns
individual lawyers commissioned as notaries who experience age-related
facultative decline and thus achieves its purpose in the individual case
(cf. para. 131), this is not the typical case. Rather, the majority of
officeholders are forced to give up their office at age 70 even though
they are still able to competently exercise their notarial duties.
Conversely, the age limit does not apply to officeholders who experience
an age-related decline in faculties before they turn 70.

dd) Under the given circumstances, the age limit of 70 years pursuant
to § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for
Notaries is no longer appropriate with regard to lawyers commissioned as
notaries and violates occupational freedom (Art. 12(1) of the Basic
Law).

The age limit constitutes a grave interference with the fundamental
right to occupational freedom. It means that lawyers commissioned as
notaries are completely prevented from further practising their
occupation, notwithstanding the possibility of sporadic work as deputies
and administrators.

At the time of its introduction, the age limit could considerably
further the purposes pursued – achieving an appropriate age structure
within the profession in the interest of the proper functioning of the
administration of justice and a fair distribution of career
opportunities – due to the increasing number of lawyers who sought to be
commissioned as notaries at the time. However, this is no longer the
case today. The factual circumstances have changed. It is still true
that the age limit pursues protected interests of the common good of
considerable weight, and that it is constitutionally suitable for
achieving these interests. A less intrusive means than the age limit is
not ascertainable. However, its significance for the purposes of the
proper functioning of the administration of justice and of a fair
distribution of career opportunities has evidently declined due to the
shortage of applicants for a commission as notary in almost all regions.
The shortage of applicants is sustained; as set out above, it has become
entrenched over many years and has been known to the legislator for some
time (cf. BTDrucks 19/26828, p. 113 f.). The number of notarial
transactions for the needs assessment can only be adjusted to a limited
extent, and it cannot fundamentally change the situation. Due to these
developments, which were not foreseeable when the law was created, the
assumptions on which the introduction of the age limit was based are no
longer correct. This means that the assessment of the legislator at the
time is now called into question and is no longer plausible (cf. BVerfGE
158, 282 <346 para. 155>). The burdens imposed on the fundamental
right holders by the challenged rule are no longer in reasonable
proportion to the significantly reduced benefits to the common good.

A different assessment on the grounds that the age limit continues to
provide protection from risks arising from officeholders with
age-related facultative decline is also not warranted. The additional
consideration of this further purpose does not qualify the rule as
appropriate. In this respect, too, the degree to which the purpose is
achieved is low, as set out above. While the legislator is in principle
entitled to create statutory age limits for typical cases, an age limit
of 70 years does not reflect the standard case of a notary who performs
inadequately, regardless of the fact that other considerations may apply
to other occupations.

Overall, the age limit of 70 for lawyers commissioned as notaries
pursuant to § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code
for Notaries is no longer appropriate and imposes unreasonable burdens
on fundamental right holders. It is unconstitutional as a result of its
violation of Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law.

1. The finding that a statutory provision is unconstitutional in
principle results in that provision being declared void. However,
pursuant to § 31(2) second and third sentence of the Federal
Constitutional Court Act, the Federal Constitutional Court may limit its
decision to declaring that an unconstitutional provision is merely
incompatible with the Constitution. It then merely objects to the
unconstitutional provision without declaring it void. The Court may
combine the declaration of incompatibility with a temporary order to
continue to apply the unconstitutional provision. This may be considered
in cases where the immediate invalidity of the objectionable provision
would eliminate the statutory basis for protecting exceptionally
significant interests of the common good, and if a balancing of these
interests against the affected fundamental rights requires that the
interference be tolerated for a transitional period (cf. BVerfGE 141,
220 <351 para. 355>; 165, 363 <440 para. 174> –
Automated data analysis; established case-law).

2. The rule imposed by § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the
Federal Notaries Act is, to the extent it applies to lawyers
commissioned as notaries, merely incompatible with the Constitution
rather than void. The age limit will be temporarily kept in place until
30 June 2026. It may no longer be applied after that date.

Declaring the rule incompatible with the Constitution avoids serious
impairments to the functionality of the administration of justice and to
the rights of the affected fundamental right holders that would arise
with a declaration of voidness. In particular, it avoids legal
uncertainty with regard to the status of those lawyers commissioned as
notaries who have already forfeited their office due to the age limit.
The continued application of the provision on a temporary basis until 30
June 2026 protects the rights of those lawyers commissioned as notaries
and officeholders associated with them (cf. § 9(2) Federal Code for
Notaries) who have already made legal and economic arrangements in the
expectation that the age limit will continue to apply. It will also
allow the justice departments of the Länder to adapt to the new
legal situation.

3. The rights of the complainant and other lawyers commissioned as
notaries who had to forfeit their office due to the age limit pursuant
to § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Code for
Notaries to reapply for vacant positions after the temporary application
of the rule expires will remain unchanged. Insofar as these persons do
not meet the statutory requirements of § 5b of the Federal Code for
Notaries, because such requirements were only introduced after they
first entered the profession, the justice departments of the
Länder will have to take into consideration that this provision
establishes target or standard requirements (cf. § 5b(1) and (4) second
sentence Federal Code for Notaries), and that they may deviate from
these requirements in the individual case.

4. The legislator is not barred from enacting new rules on a
mandatory age limit for lawyers commissioned as notaries. It follows
from the considerations above that the legislator is afforded broad
leeway for designing such an age limit in conformity with the
Constitution. For example, the legislator may introduce an age limit
that only applies to certain regions, a higher age limit or a provision
that ties the continued exercise of notarial office to the faculties of
the officeholder.

Despite the incompatibility of the current age limit rule with
Art. 12(1) of the Basic Law, the judgment of the Federal Court of
Justice challenged by the constitutional complaint is still valid, as
the rule will continue in place subject to the aforementioned conditions
(cf. BVerfGE 158, 282 <388 Rn. 261>; 166, 196 <289 Rn. 247>
– Remuneration of prisoner labour II). The
constitutional complaint is therefore unsuccessful to the extent that it
is directed against that decision (cf. BVerfGE 115, 276
<319>).

--- SUMMARY ---

1.	Occupational freedom entails both an economic dimension and a dimension related to the development of one’s personality. It gives definition to the fundamental right to the free development of one’s personality in the area of individual achievement and creating and earning a livelihood and aims to have the least amount of regulation of occupational activity possible.

2.	Given the sustained shortage of lawyers commissioned as notaries and the current knowledge on how age affects professional faculties, the age limit of 70 years pursuant to § 47 no. 2, first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Notaries Act only achieves the objectives pursued by such rule – the proper functioning of the administration of justice, the (fair) distribution of career opportunities among generations and the protection of the administration of justice from risks that may arise from age-related decline in faculties among notaries – to a very limited extent. It disproportionately restricts occupational freedom insofar as it concerns lawyers commissioned as notaries.

I. The applicant – a lawyer commissioned as a notary public in North Rhine-Westphalia – inter alia, challenges the statutory rule in § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Federal Notaries Act (hereinafter, the “Act”), according to which the office of notary public expires upon reaching the age limit of 70 years of age both for notaries practicing primarily as such and for lawyers commissioned as notaries. The applicant alleges that the age limit violates his occupational freedom under Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law.

II. Based on the considerations below, the Federal Constitutional Court held that the constitutional complaint is well-founded insofar as it challenges the age limit rule. This limit is incompatible with Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law insofar as it relates to lawyers commissioned as notaries. The age limit disproportionately interferes with the occupational freedom of lawyers commissioned as notaries.

The age limit nevertheless serves constitutionally legitimate objectives. It is intended to ensure an appropriate age structure for notaries public that serves the interest of the effective administration of justice. An age limit is also a means to provide an appropriate distribution of career opportunities among generations; this constitutes a legitimate objective for employment and social policy. Finally, it serves the legitimate objective of protecting the administration of justice from risks that may arise due to age-related diminution of function among notaries public.

Despite changing factual circumstances, the age limit remains suitable and necessary under constitutional law to achieve the legislative objectives.

However, the age limit is no longer proportionate in the strict sense with regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries. It places an unreasonable burden on the holders of fundamental rights.

The intensity of the interference is severe. The age limit is a rule that affects the choice of occupation. Upon reaching this limit, the commission as notary public expires. As the decisive criteria is impossible to avoid, the holders of fundamental rights have no influence on the end of their career nor can they change it to suit their own circumstances. The law does not provide for exceptions. The interference affects both areas of protection guaranteed by occupational freedom, i.e. obtaining an economic livelihood and developing one’s personality.

The interference is mitigated to some extent by the possibility for those that must forfeit their office due to the age limit to remain active in the same field as a substitute (Notarvertreter) or temporary (Notariatsverwalter) notary public or to resume their primary occupation as a lawyer. Nevertheless, the degree of mitigation is very limited.

Seventy years is a substantially later age than the standard retirement age set by law at which civil servants and others may claim full retirement benefits. On its own, however, this does not result in a mitigation of the intensity of the interference. Lawyers commissioned as notaries public – much like professionals in higher positions in the civil service and other occupations requiring higher education – generally begin this occupation at a relatively late age. In addition, in looking at society as a whole, career and job application paths have become more flexible and interruptions in the progression from education to a career to retirement have become increasingly more common.

This must be weighed against the interests of the common good, which also have considerable weight. The proper functioning of the administration of justice – namely, providing high quality notarial services carried out by competent, sufficiently experienced notaries of various ages – is of great importance both on an individual level and for society as a whole. Equally important is the social and employment policy objective of the legislator to provide a fair distribution of career opportunities among generations. These objectives also serve to give effect to the rights of potential notaries under Art. 12.1 of the Basic Law to enter into the profession.

With regard to lawyers commissioned as notaries, the age limit provides a very limited contribution towards these objectives. Given the sustained shortage of lawyers commissioned as notaries and the current knowledge on how age influences professional competence, the age limit only achieves the objectives pursued by such rule to a very limited extent.

The burdens imposed on the fundamental rights holders by the challenged rule are therefore not in reasonable proportion to the significantly reduced benefits to the common good.

The rule imposed by § 47 no. 2 first alternative and § 48a of the Act is, to the extent it applies to lawyers commissioned as notaries, incompatible with the Constitution rather than void. The age limit will be temporarily kept in place until 30 June 2026. It may no longer be applied after that date.
